---
title: "Employee Attrition Analysis with R"
output: html_notebook
author: @nguyenduc024
updated date: 03/12/2026
---

This is an [R Markdown](http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com) Notebook. When you execute code within the notebook, the results appear beneath the code.

Try executing this chunk by clicking the *Run* button within the chunk or by placing your cursor inside it and pressing *Ctrl+Shift+Enter*.

`{rm(list = ls())}`

Add a new chunk by clicking the *Insert Chunk* button on the toolbar or by pressing *Ctrl+Alt+I*.

When you save the notebook, an HTML file containing the code and output will be saved alongside it (click the *Preview* button or press *Ctrl+Shift+K* to preview the HTML file).

The preview shows you a rendered HTML copy of the contents of the editor. Consequently, unlike *Knit*, *Preview* does not run any R code chunks. Instead, the output of the chunk when it was last run in the editor is displayed.

# Setup dataset

## Install library

```{r}
install.packages(c("tidyverse", "janitor", "scales", "corrplot"))

library(tidyverse)
library(janitor)

```

```{r}
# Read dataset
df <- read.csv("C:/Users/kirat/Desktop/PROJECT - Employee_Attrition_Analysis/data/Employee Attrition.csv", sep = ";", check.names = FALSE) %>%
```

```{r}
# Show top 6 rows
glimpse(df)
head(df)
```

# Exploratory Data Analysis

## Chart 1: The "Burnout" Heatmap (Work Hours vs. Satisfaction)

```{r}
# Advanced Density Heatmap
ggplot(df, aes(x = average_montly_hours, y = satisfaction_level)) +
  geom_bin2d(bins = 30) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "inferno") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 250, linetype = "dashed", color = "white") + # Highlight high hours
  labs(title = "The Burnout Zone: Hours vs. Satisfaction",
       subtitle = "Employees working > 250h/month show distinct clusters of low satisfaction",
       x = "Average Monthly Hours", y = "Satisfaction Level") +
  theme_minimal()
```

##Char 2: Average working hours by department

```{r}
library(tidyverse)

# Function to fix the comma-to-dot decimal and convert to numeric
fix_to_numeric <- function(x) {
  as.numeric(str_replace(x, ",", "."))
}

df_clean %>%
  group_by(dept) %>%
  summarise(avg_hours = mean(average_montly_hours, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(dept, avg_hours), y = avg_hours)) +
  geom_col(fill = "steelblue") +
  coord_flip() +

  # expand_limits(y = ...) ensures there is empty space at the end of the bars
  geom_text(aes(label = round(avg_hours, 1)), 
            hjust = -0.5, 
            size = 4.2) + 
  expand_limits(y = max(df_clean$average_montly_hours, na.rm = TRUE) * 1) + 
  labs(title = "Average Monthly Hours per Department",
       x = "Department", y = "Average Hours") +
  theme_minimal()
```

##Chart 3: Satisfaction Distribution (The "Morale Check")

```{r}
ggplot(df, aes(x = satisfaction_level)) +
  geom_density(fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.5) +
  labs(title = "Employee Satisfaction Density",
       x = "Satisfaction Level (0 - 1)", y = "Density") +
  theme_minimal()
```

##Chart 4: Correlation Matrix Heatmap

```{r}
corrplot(cor_data, 
         method = "color",     # This fills the entire cell with color
         type = "full",        # Ensures the full square matrix
         diag = TRUE,          # Includes the diagonal
         tl.col = "black",     # Label text color
         tl.srt = 45,          # Label rotation
         addCoef.col = "black",# Text color for the correlation coefficients
         number.cex = 0.7,     # Size of the text
         col = colorRampPalette(c("#E74C3C", "white", "#3498DB"))(200))
```

##Chart 5: Employee Tenue by Department

```{r}
# Visualizing count of employees by tenure and department
df_clean %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = as.factor(time_spend_company), fill = dept)) +
  geom_bar(position = "stack") + 
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set3") + # Professional color palette
  labs(title = "Employee Tenure Distribution by Department",
       subtitle = "Total headcount per year of service, broken down by department",
       x = "Years at Company", 
       y = "Number of Employees",
       fill = "Department") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

# Key Insights

-   Employees working more than 250 hours per month tend to show lower satisfaction levels.
-   Moderate workloads are associated with higher employee satisfaction.
-   Most employees leave the company after their third year.
-   Technical, Sales and Product Manager departments consistently work longer hours than others.

# Recommendations

1.  Implement career development programs for Mid-Tenure employees
2.  Introduce performance-Based incentives around the 3-Year milestone
3.  Monitor Workload to prevent burnout
4.  Conduct Periodic Employee Satisfaction Surveys
